abstracts.title: 1. Review advancements in NPWT, including novel peel-and-place dressings, and apply an algorithmic approach to selecting appropriate technologies
abstracts.abstract:
1. Review advancements in NPWT, including novel peel-and-place dressings, and apply an algorithmic approach to selecting appropriate technologies

2. Analyze the clinical utility of an integrated peel-and-place NPWT dressing in promoting wound healing, reducing dressing changes, and enhancing patient comfort

3. Evaluate the importance of periwound skin protection and review the science and clinical application of polymer cyanoacrylate–based skin protectants for managing and protecting at-risk skin under NPWT

4. Examine case-based scenarios highlighting the components of the NPWT toolkit, and demonstrate the step-down therapies involved in progressing toward wound closure

abstracts.title: This hands-on, experiential workshop delivers an in-depth exploration of the latest advancements in wound care—from hospital to clinic to home. Designed for the interdisciplinary wound care team, the session integrates the full spectrum of innovation in negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT), compression therapy, advanced dressings, and skin care.  Through engaging case-based discussions, interactive polling, and immersive hands-on stations, participants will experience how technology and clinical expertise intersect to optimize outcomes across diverse care settings. Expert faculty will guide learners through each phase of the patient journey, emphasizing practical strategies for intervention, and continuity of care.

abstracts.title: WHS Welcome and Introduction
WHS Session A: Thomas K. Hunt Honorary Lecture
abstracts.abstract:
Biomaterials for Modeling and Therapeutic Delivery in Wound Healing and Repair

 Jason A. Burdick, PhD

The Thomas K. Hunt Endowed Lecture, established in 2013, honors the legacy of Dr. Thomas K. Hunt, a pioneering leader in wound healing research and a founding member of the Wound Healing Society. This annual lecture aims to inspire and educate wound healing researchers by highlighting innovations in related fields. Each year, the Wound Healing Foundation selects an honorary speaker, not limited to those directly involved in wound healing research but inclusive of investigators who demonstrate the ability to bridge scientific gaps and apply fundamental physiological principles to understand healing processes across multiple organ systems.

abstracts.title: The ideal response to injury is regeneration, but mature tissues typically have limited capacity for true regeneration. A better understanding of the mechanisms by which tissue regeneration occurs, including how cell-ECM interactions and mechanosignaling influence this process, could lead to new approaches to promote regenerative healing. This session will highlight new work on engineered biomimetic model development and detailed characterization of ECM-driven responses of cells from regenerative species to uncover novel mechanistic information about tissue regeneration.

abstracts.title: This session delves into the dynamic cross-talk between microbes and the immune system that determines whether wounds heal or persist in inflammation. Presentations will highlight how shifts in the wound microbiome reshape repair processes and how cytotoxic and regulatory immune cell responses can both defend against and drive tissue damage. Together, these talks by this collaborative team uncover emerging principles of immune–microbial balance with broad implications for infection control and chronic wound management.

abstracts.title: Innovation Theater Lunch: Enhancing Skin Substitutes: Type-I Collagen Matrix for Safety and Efficacy (non-accredited/non-approved for CECH)
abstracts.abstract:
This innovation theater session will highlight the scientific principles behind Type-I collagen biomaterials used in skin substitutes, emphasizing how high-purity, native collagen structure and biochemical modification enhance cellular interaction, bioactivity, and tissue regeneration. It also presents clinical evidence showing improved wound-healing outcomes with advanced collagen matrices such as Helicoll, demonstrating their potential to accelerate healing and improve regenerative performance.

Sponsored by Encoll.

abstracts.title: This dynamic educational experience highlights the latest surgical techniques and advanced strategies for complex wound management and limb preservation. Guided by the T.I.M.E. Principles, the program integrates high-impact didactic lectures, in-depth case reviews, and an immersive cadaveric bio-skills laboratory to bridge theory with real-world application.  Participants will move beyond passive learning to actively refine surgical decision-making and technical skills in a hands-on environment. Educational content will emphasize reconstructive tissue repair, advanced closure strategies, and limb salvage techniques—equipping clinicians with practical, evidence-based tools they can immediately implement in complex wound care scenarios. By combining expert faculty insight, case-based discussion, and cadaver lab experience, this activity delivers a comprehensive and clinically relevant approach to optimizing outcomes in patients with challenging wounds.

abstracts.title: This session explores how inflammatory cues are precisely orchestrated to determine wound outcomes. Michel Enamorado, PhD will examine how neural and immune pathways intersect to coordinate inflammation and repair. Complementing this, the second presentation by Brett Shook, PhD, will discuss the dynamic cellular and molecular networks that drive immune activation and resolution after injury, highlighting how spatial and temporal signaling regulate myeloid cell function and how these processes go awry in impaired healing such as diabetes. Together, these talks will provide new insights into the complex regulatory mechanisms that define successful versus pathological healing.

abstracts.title: This session examines how nutritional status and circadian rhythms shape epithelial function and resilience. Presentations will explore how feeding–fasting cycles and the body’s internal clock influence skin physiology, repair, and metabolic balance, as well as how circadian disruption compromises mucosal barrier integrity, predisposing to inflammation and systemic disease. Together, these insights reveal shared mechanisms through which time and metabolism coordinate epithelial health across organ systems.

abstracts.title: WHS Session G: Forces that Scar: Mechanobiology Across Fibrotic Tissues
abstracts.abstract:
Tissue injury activates a cascade of signaling pathways to recruit different cell types to orchestrate the healing response. However, excessive cell activation, inflammation, and accumulation of extracellular matrix can lead to inert, dysfunctional scar tissue as seen in pulmonary fibrosis, myocardial infarction, and hypertrophic scar formation. Mechanical tension plays a central role in orchestrating fibroproliferative responses following injury, particularly through mechanosensitive signaling pathways. In this session, investigators will discuss how mechanical signaling pathways can regulate fibrosis across various organ systems.

Stiffness and Solid Stress in Liver Fibrosis
 
Rebecca G. Wells, MD

Matrix Mechanobiology Regulates Pro-Fibrotic Cardiac Fibroblast Activation
 
Adam J. Engler, PhD

abstracts.title: Innovation Theater Breakfast: The Science of Slough (non-accredited)
abstracts.abstract:
Despite being a common and clinically challenging feature of acute and chronic wounds, slough remains an understudied component of wound pathology. Its presence obscures wound assessment, perpetuates inflammation and microbial burden, and delays granulation and epithelialization- making effective slough management central to wound bed preparation and healing. Historically, research has focused more heavily on infection, biofilm, and necrosis, leaving critical gaps in our understanding of slough’s composition, behavior, and clinical significance. This knowledge gap is no longer acceptable. Advancing outcomes demand sharper science, clearer frameworks for assessment, and technologies purpose-built to actively address slough as a distinct and meaningful barrier to healing.

During this Innovation Theater, an interactive panel featuring Joyce Black, PhD, RN, FAAN; Lindsay Kalan, PhD; and Kristina Fawcett, DNP, NP-C, CWS will challenge the status quo by unpacking key insights from the IWII 2023 What is Slough? document, exploring slough’s heterogeneity and its emerging role as a marker of stalled healing, and translating evolving science into bedside decision-making. The discussion will examine practical solution pathways, including research-driven technologies within Urgo Medical’s portfolio, designed to actively target slough and advance wound bed preparation. Together, this session aims to reposition slough- not as a passive bystander- but as a critical, actionable target in modern wound care, demanding smarter science, advance tools, and faster clinical action.

Sponsored by Urgo Medical North America

abstracts.title: Current international clinical practice guidelines recommend the use of multilayer foam dressings on high-risk body areas, such as the sacrum and heels in individuals at risk for pressure injury, as well as for the treatment of pressure injuries and other wounds.
abstracts.abstract:
Current international clinical practice guidelines recommend the use of multilayer foam dressings on high-risk body areas, such as the sacrum and heels in individuals at risk for pressure injury, as well as for the treatment of pressure injuries and other wounds.

This session will examine results from recent peer-reviewed publications exploring new, advanced testing methods developed for evaluating dressing efficacy in pressure injury prevention (PIP), with a focus on reducing skin exposure to shear and excess heat, as well as the performance of this portfolio of next-generation multilayered foam dressings in the management of hard-to-heal wounds.

abstracts.title: Opening Ceremony
Keynote Address: Beyond Gravity: How Spaceflight Transforms the Human Body
abstracts.abstract: What happens to the human body when it leaves Earth? Spaceflight challenges astronauts and affects fundamental biological processes. Microgravity and radiation alter immunity, tissue repair, and cellular biology in ways that provide new insights into physiology. In this keynote, NASA astronaut and scientist Dr. Kate Rubins shares her extraordinary journey, from spacewalking outside the International Space Station to becoming the first person to sequence DNA in microgravity. Her pioneering research demonstrates how pushing biology to its limits in space unlocks discoveries with powerful implications for medicine, innovation, and health on Earth.

abstracts.title: Wrapped Up in Choices: Rethinking Compression for VLUs
abstracts.abstract: Compression therapy is essential in managing VLUs, yet terminology and application remain inconsistent. This session redefines compression language, translates evidence into clinical practice, and explores the full spectrum of modalities—from toe wraps to advanced systems. Through case-based examples, clinicians will learn how to tailor compression strategies across the continuum of care to improve outcomes and adherence.

abstracts.title: DFU: Offloading, Surgery and Beyond
abstracts.abstract: This session will discuss the surgical reconstruction of the diabetic foot. Topics include prophylactic surgery to prevent ulcer formation, procedures addressing the wound and its underlying etiology, and strategies for preventing recurrence. Attendees will review both office-based and OR-based bone and soft tissue procedures. Functional amputations, as well as Charcot reconstruction, will also be covered.

abstracts.title: Social Media: The Promise and Problems
abstracts.abstract: Social media has rapidly evolved into a powerful platform for healthcare professionals to amplify their voices, grow their practices, and share credible, evidence-based information with broad audiences. This session will explore how wound care providers can use social media to support clinical education, empower patients, and potentially improve healing outcomes. It will also address the potential pitfalls, including misinformation, professionalism concerns, and digital fatigue. This session will provide real-world insights into responsible and effective digital engagement in healthcare.

abstracts.title: Common Challenges in Drug Development for Non-Healing Chronic Wounds: Insights from FDA and WCCC Milestones
abstracts.abstract: Chronic wound therapeutics face significant regulatory challenges, with many promising treatments failing to receive FDA approval despite positive preclinical data. This session will explore the most common regulatory pitfalls encountered during drug development for non-healing chronic wounds, drawing on FDA CDER's review experience and insights from the Wound Care Collaborative Community, WCCC. Attendees will learn about critical errors in clinical trial design, endpoint selection, and regulatory strategy that often hinder development programs. Understanding these frequent failure points can help developers design more robust trials and increase their chances of regulatory success in this challenging field. Additionally, attendees will learn about the substantial contributions of the WCCC in promoting innovation and enhancing patient access to advanced wound care.

abstracts.title: Every year, this session brings together three sister societies (ETRS, EWMA and WHS) to focus on specific topic. This year this session will focus on inflammation as a central regulator of wound healing across molecular, cellular, and clinical contexts. Through complementary topics, the speakers will explore how dysregulated immune response shapes signaling pathways that control keratinocyte behavior, drive pathophysiology and influence damage-associated molecular pattern signaling (DAMP). Together, these presentations will highlight shared mechanisms and emerging concepts that provide novel insights and potential approaches to management, therapies and diagnostics targeting inflammation.

abstracts.abstract:
HMP Education has designated an unrestricted grant from MTF Biologics.

4. Describe key reimbursement considerations associated with the use of aseptically processed, acellular human dermal grafts and placental membranes.

abstracts.title: Innovation Theater Lunch: Revolutionary Technologies in Wound Care: Skin-Friendly Tissue Adhesives & Next-Gen NPWT Systems (non-accredited/non-approved for CECH)
abstracts.abstract:
This dynamic innovation luncheon will highlight new wound care technologies designed to support optimal healing outcomes, skin integrity, and care efficiency. Expert clinicians and researchers will discuss the science and clinical application of recently FDA‑cleared, skin‑friendly tissue adhesives and novel film dressing technologies, with a focus on atraumatic skin closure, fixation, and skin compatibility. The session will also review recently FDA‑cleared, next‑generation NPWT systems, emphasizing pressure and exudate management, usability, and efficiencies across the hospital‑to‑home care ecosystem.

Sponsored by DeRoyal Industries, Inc.

abstracts.title: Lower extremity wounds are among the most complex and costly to treat, demanding not only a deep understanding of pathophysiology but also mastery of advanced, evidence-based interventions. In this highly interactive workshop, participants will navigate real-world cases while engaging in hands-on demonstrations of cutting-edge therapies such as skin substitutes, compression therapy, advanced dressings, topical oxygen therapy, debridement devices, and negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT). This immersive format provides a rare opportunity to practice techniques under expert guidance, reinforcing both confidence and clinical skill.

abstracts.title: CVI Management: From Meds to Surgery
abstracts.abstract: Compression therapy is the cornerstone of CVI treatment—but what other interventions should be added to address the condition holistically? This session will review additional state-of-the-art treatments for CVI, including medications/venoactive agents, therapeutic exercise, and endovascular or surgical interventions.

abstracts.title: Panel: Multidisciplinary Approach to DFU
abstracts.abstract: This session will explore collaborative strategies used by a multidisciplinary team including a Doctor of Podiatric Medicine (DPM), physical therapist, and nurse. Each specialty contributes distinct expertise—from wound care and rehabilitation to patient education and psychosocial support. The lecture will highlight how integrated care models improve healing outcomes, reduce amputation risk, and increase patient engagement through coordinated interventions. Using case studies and practical discussion, attendees will gain insights into implementing multidisciplinary frameworks for managing diabetic foot ulcers.

abstracts.title: Smoking and Drugs in Healing
abstracts.abstract: Substance use, including tobacco and cannabis, adversely affects wound healing through well-established pathophysiological mechanisms. Furthermore, many patients inquire about cannabis for pain control. This session will address evidence-based strategies for managing wounds in patients with tobacco use disorders, emphasizing interdisciplinary collaboration, goal-oriented care planning, and practical interventions beyond traditional cessation counseling. As wound care providers, we play a critical role in optimizing outcomes by addressing modifiable risk factors within a comprehensive treatment framework.

abstracts.title: Dermatology Consults
abstracts.abstract: As it turns out, there is skin around wounds (who knew?), and our patients often complain of rashes and growths. Additionally, some wound etiologies cannot be understood without recognizing the underlying dermatological disease. In this session, cases of skin disease in people with wounds will be discussed by both a wound expert and a dermatologist to help unlock the mystery.

abstracts.title: Lymphedema Management
abstracts.abstract: Lymphedema is a chronic condition resulting from impaired lymphatic physiology, leading to fluid accumulation, inflammation, and tissue remodeling. This session will explore the evolving landscape of lymphedema management—including pharmacologic therapies, compression techniques, and surgical interventions—highlighting which treatments are currently effective, which show promise, and what advancements may be on the horizon. Attendees will gain a comprehensive understanding of both the underlying pathophysiology and practical treatment strategies to improve patient outcomes.

abstracts.abstract: This session will provide valuable tools for creating a virtual interdisciplinary team. Studies have shown that patient outcomes are improved when care is managed by an interdisciplinary team. By leveraging technology, it is possible to develop a collaborative care team that extends beyond the traditional boundaries of a specific care setting or geographic location.

abstracts.title: Preventing Wounds from Occurring and Reoccurring
abstracts.abstract: This session will explore the importance of achieving the right balance in mobility to adequately strengthen new tissue without overdoing it and causing recurrence. The discussion will emphasize strategies for maintaining wound remission once wounds are closed and patients resume regular activity.

abstracts.title: Understanding the Reconstructive Ladder in Modern Wound Healing: Evolving Concepts and Collaborative Care Strategies
abstracts.abstract: Modern innovations in wound care—such as skin substitutes, NPWT, and antimicrobial therapies—are reshaping the traditional reconstructive ladder by enabling earlier and less invasive interventions. This session highlights how these technologies empower interprofessional wound care teams to manage complex wounds more effectively, often reducing the need for advanced surgical procedures. By integrating these tools into care strategies, clinicians can accelerate healing, improve outcomes, and preserve future surgical options.

abstracts.title: Accredited Industry-Supported Symposium: Reeling in Results: Clinical Applications of Fish Skin Grafts in Chronic and Atypical Wounds
abstracts.abstract:
HMP Education has designated an unrestricted grant from Kerecis.



Learning Objectives:

1. Describe the biological properties and mechanisms of action of fish skin grafts (FSGs), emphasizing how they support wound healing across a range of clinical indications.

2. Evaluate the clinical evidence supporting the use of FSGs, including illustrative case studies that demonstrate successful patient outcomes.

3. Examine the combined use of fish skin grafts and silicone dressings in wound management, differentiating their roles in tissue regeneration and exudate management, and evaluating their impact on healing outcomes and periwound skin protection in chronic and complex wounds.

4. Explore illustrative clinical case outcomes using fish skin grafts to manage chronic and atypical wounds.

5. Identify key reimbursement considerations and documentation requirements for the appropriate use of fish skin grafts in wound care.

abstracts.title: This symposium brings together experienced wound care physicians to discuss how to navigate a rapidly changing reimbursement environment while maintaining clinically appropriate decision-making in complex wounds. Faculty will highlight the clinical differentiation of Miro3D as a conformable, three-dimensional scaffold designed to address wounds with complex geometry - including depth, undermining, and tunneling - where surface dimensions alone may not reflect true wound burden. Speakers will present real-world cases and measurement and documentation practices that support consistent clinical decision-making and clear documentation in today’s environment. The session will also include updates on emerging Miro3D evidence, including randomized clinical trials and registry development, highlighting how evidence generation can guide best practices during a period of market change.

abstracts.title: Innovation Theater Breakfast: Trusted Science. New Format. Expanded Impact. Clinical Benefits of Pure Hypochlorous Acid in Gel Form (non-accredited/non-approved for CECH)
abstracts.abstract:
Modern wound care continues to present significant clinical challenges, including rapid bioburden reformation, pH imbalance, incomplete or inadequate debridement, and the escalating concern of antimicrobial resistance. These factors often complicate wound bed preparation and delay healing progression.

This session introduces a newly available hypochlorous acid gel designed to address these challenges through sustained activity and extended contact time at the wound surface. Compared to traditional solution formats, the gel provides prolonged antimicrobial activity, wound acidification, and debridement while remaining in place to support an optimal healing environment.

Insights from a newly published multidisciplinary expert panel review will be presented, highlighting real-world clinical experiences from physicians and nurse practitioners who evaluated the gel’s advantages, limitations, and potential role in both acute and chronic wound management. The discussion will place the gel within the broader context of available topical therapies, acknowledging the strengths and trade-offs of each approach.

Through evidence-based clinical case reviews across diverse wound types and care settings, this presentation will demonstrate how one trusted hypochlorous acid solution—now available in a sustained gel format—can streamline protocols, reduce unnecessary product layering, and support effective, accessible healing outcomes.

A new era in primary wound care is here! 

Sponsored by Urgo Medical North America.

abstracts.title: Accredited Industry-Supported Symposium: Defining the Future of Autologous Wound Care: Clinical Evidence, Reimbursement, and Practice Integration of a Multilayered Leukocyte, Platelet, and Fibrin Patch
abstracts.abstract:
HMP Education has designated an unrestricted grant from Reapplix.



Learning Objectives:

1. Interpret clinical evidence and real-world retrospective outcomes data on the use of an autologous multilayered leukocyte, platelet, and fibrin patch for hard-to-heal wounds and differentiate this patient-derived, point-of-care technology from other autologous blood-derived products.

2. Demonstrate how an autologous multilayered leukocyte, platelet, and fibrin patch can be efficiently incorporated into existing clinic workflows without disruption while maintaining operational efficiency in outpatient wound care practice.

3. Assess reimbursement pathways, coverage considerations, and documentation requirements for the use of an autologous multilayered leukocyte, platelet, and fibrin patch in the office or outpatient wound care setting.

4. Evaluate where an autologous multilayered leukocyte, platelet, and fibrin patch fits within the overall wound care treatment armamentarium, and consider whether a patient biology-derived therapy should be used as a first-line option for chronic wounds.

5. Explore real-world case studies of chronic wounds treated in the office or outpatient wound care setting with an autologous multilayered leukocyte, platelet, and fibrin patch.

abstracts.title: Accredited Industry-Supported Symposium: Climbing Smarter, Not Higher: Optimizing the Wound Reconstruction Ladder Using Hypochlorous Acid–Based Products and Highly Charged Fiber Dressings
abstracts.abstract:
HMP Education has designated an unrestricted grant from Urgo Medical North America. 


Learning Objectives:

1. Describe the wound reconstruction ladder and explain how early intervention can prevent progression to higher, more complex, and more costly reconstructive treatments.

2. Review the clinical application of pure hypochlorous acid–preserved cleansers, and wound management gel primary dressings, as well as negatively charged fiber dressings in supporting continuous debridement of slough as part of wound bed preparation.

3. Explain the evidence how wound surface pH and a physiologically acidic wound environment promotes healing by enhancing oxygen release and limiting bacterial proliferation in chronic or non-healing wounds.

4. Examine how early intervention using pure hypochlorous acid–preserved cleansers and gels and negatively charged fiber dressings may prevent escalation to higher, more complex levels of the wound reconstruction ladder.

5. Explore clinical cases demonstrating outcomes achieved through the early use of hypochlorous preserved cleansers and negatively charged fibers to promote wound healing without unnecessary escalation along the wound reconstruction ladder.

abstracts.title: Accredited Workshop: Wound Infection Day: Consensus and Controversies in Contamination and Bioburden - The Battle Against Slough and Microbial Burden
abstracts.abstract:
HMP Education has designated an unrestricted grant from Urgo Foundation. 


Learning objectives:

• Explain the patient-centric drivers of wound infection today

• Determine if there are gradations of contamination that can be agreed upon, categorize the levels of microbes in the wound, and differentiate modes of treatment for wounds contaminated at various levels

• Assess current techniques, in both inpatient and outpatient settings, to determine levels of contamination and identify which method to use and when

• Determine the relationship between slough removal and infection control

• Explain the role of antimicrobial agents (antibiotics, antimicrobial barriers, antimicrobial preserved cleansers)  and how to critically assess their efficacy in the management of bioburden

• Examine new and emerging technologies for slough/debris/microbe removal, including hypochlorous acid-based cleansers and charged fiber technology for physical removal of slough and debridement support/amplification

• Explore illustrative case studies on wound infection/slough management and apply current strategies

abstracts.title: GLP-1 Agonists, Pharmacology, and Wound Healing
abstracts.abstract: This session will delve into the complex interplay between systemic medications and the wound healing process. Attendees will learn to identify common pharmacologic agents that can positively or negatively impact wound closure, tissue regeneration, and infection control. The session will place particular focus on the emerging role of GLP-1 agonists in improving wound outcomes and supporting amputation prevention strategies.
abstracts.abstract_html: This session will delve into the complex interplay between systemic medications and the wound healing process. Attendees will learn to identify common pharmacologic agents that can positively or negatively impact wound closure, tissue regeneration, and infection control. The session will place particular focus on the emerging role of GLP-1 agonists in improving wound outcomes and supporting amputation prevention strategies.

abstracts.title: Skin Substitutes and Blood-Derived Products: Working Together for Patient Outcomes
abstracts.abstract: This session will explore the wide range of therapies available to assist with wound progression and healing. Faculty will assess how to choose the most effective treatment plan for best patient outcomes. The use of skin substitute products and autologous tissue options such as skin grafts, flap transfers, and other biological materials like blood or adipose tissue will be discussed.

The use of skin substitute products and autologous tissue options such as skin grafts, flap transfers, and other biological materials like blood or adipose tissue will be discussed.

abstracts.title: Wound Dressings: What Do We Need to Know?
abstracts.abstract: This session will discuss the primary categories of wound dressings and propose fundamental principles for their utilization, combinations, and access in real-world practice. While often considered a basic aspect of wound care, dressings can subtly alter the wound environment in ways that significantly impact healing. Attendees will gain a deeper understanding of how to optimize dressing selection and use.

abstracts.title: Wound Care Business Navigator: The ABCs of Wound Care Business - Understanding Coding & Compliance
abstracts.abstract: This session will provide a foundational introduction to wound care reimbursement, explaining how healthcare providers should report the services they deliver. The session will trace the patient journey from the treatment room (documentation and scheduling) to the back office (coding and claim submission). Attendees will learn the basic "ABCs" of how wound care claims should be submitted, gaining a clearer understanding of the financial pathway for their clinics.

abstracts.title: The Wound Microenvironment: What to Know, What to Do
abstracts.abstract: The wound microenvironment is made up of various cells, enzymes, cytokines, growth factors and microbiome that function within a dynamic and ever-changing extracellular matrix. This session will examine the differences between normal and abnormal microenvironments, how to evaluate them, and what current and emerging therapies are available to support healing in chronic wounds. Factors such as inflammation, microbial presence, and oxygenation will be discussed.

Factors such as inflammation, microbial presence, and oxygenation will be discussed.

abstracts.title: What's New in Predicting Healing
abstracts.abstract: This session will review how, despite significant efforts over the years, point-of-care diagnostics and predictive tools for chronic wounds remain underdeveloped. Attendees will discuss recent innovations that go beyond traditional assessments such as wound size and duration. These include the potential use of molecular and chemical signatures, as well as machine learning approaches, to predict or monitor clinical outcomes.

abstracts.title: Wound Care Business Navigator: AI and Wound Care
abstracts.abstract: This session will introduce attendees to Artificial Intelligence (AI) and its emerging role in everyday wound care. We'll explore how AI can support your clinic—using smart tools to measure wounds, organize patient information, and streamline workflow—making your work more efficient and allowing more time for direct patient care. Come learn how AI isn’t just for science fiction; it’s becoming a practical and helpful tool in wound care practice.

abstracts.title: Surgery and Drugs for HS and PG
abstracts.abstract: Patients with hidradenitis suppurativa (HS) are increasingly arriving in wound care clinics asking about new drugs and procedures. This session will review the newest HS medications and surgical approaches for tunneling wounds. We will also highlight pyoderma gangrenosum (PG), covering key therapies, diagnostic challenges, and management strategies. Attendees will gain practical updates to confidently guide patients seeking advanced care for these complex dermatologic wounds.

abstracts.title: WHS Session M: Extracellular Vesicles
abstracts.abstract:
Extracellular vesicles (EVs) have emerged as potent mediators of intercellular communication and tissue repair. This session will highlight cutting-edge research on the molecular and therapeutic roles of EVs in wound healing, from their involvement in inflammation and immune modulation to their development as engineered regenerative therapeutics. Speakers will discuss recent discoveries on how EVs orchestrate repair processes in chronic and acute wounds, offering new perspectives for translational applications in regenerative medicine.

abstracts.title: How to Help Older Adults Heal: Science to Practice
abstracts.abstract: Older adults face unique challenges in healing due to age-related physiological changes, chronic conditions, and social factors that can delay recovery. Bridging the gap between scientific advances and everyday clinical practice is essential to improving outcomes. This presentation will emphasize translation of current research into age-appropriate evidence-based strategies that promote wound healing, functional recovery, and overall well-being in older adults. Incorporating the 4Ms: What Matters, Medication, Mentation and Mobility provides a patient-centered framework that clinicians and caregivers can apply to enhance resilience and quality of life in this population.

Innovation Theater Breakfast: From Acute Burns to Chronic Ulcers: The Expanding Role of Bromelain-Based Enzymatic Debridement (non-accredited/non-approved for CECH)
abstracts.abstract:
EscharEx® is a novel biological drug in late-stage clinical development for venous leg ulcers. It consists of a complex mixture of proteolytic enzymes enriched with bromelain, derived from the pineapple stem, enabling rapid and selective removal of non-viable tissue commonly present in hard-to-heal wounds. Emerging evidence also suggests biological effects beyond debridement, including promotion of granulation tissue, anti-inflammatory activity, and stimulation of cell migration. EscharEx is specifically formulated for chronic wounds and is based on the same active ingredient as NexoBrid®, an approved drug in the US, EU and Japan, for eschar removal in deep burns and widely adopted in burn centers worldwide.


This symposium will review the evolving mechanism of action of bromelain-based enzymatic therapy and its expansion from acute burn care to chronic wound management. Clinical data and case studies across acute injuries and hard-to-heal wounds will be presented, along with updates on the ongoing global VALUE Phase III trial in venous leg ulcers and planned studies in diabetic foot ulcers and pressure injuries.


Sponsored by MediWound.

abstracts.title: This workshop will focus on equipping clinicians with actionable insights for improving wound assessment and treatment using a multimodal imaging approach. While clinicians understand the importance of assessing bacterial burden, wound size, inflammation and perfusion, integrating these factors into real time clinical decision making can be challenging. This session aims to bridge that gap by demonstrating how a single imaging system can provide essential diagnostic information at the point of care to guide clinical decision making and improve wound care outcomes.

abstracts.title: Session Highlights:
abstracts.abstract:
Session Highlights:

• Real World Case Studies: Clinicians will review complex wound cases with mixed etiologies to see how multimodal imaging supports assessment and treatment decisions.
• Bacterial Fluorescence in Practice: Learn how real-time fluorescence imaging guides interventions and informs debridement.
• Multimodal Imaging Insights: Explore how integrated wound measurement and thermal imaging enhance assessment, documentation, and clinical workflow.
• CAMPs Optimization: Understand how multimodal imaging can improve wound bed preparation and monitoring to support the effective use and documentation of cellular and acellular matrix products.
• Evidence Based Practice: Review the recent FDA-qualified Medical Measurement Device Tool (MDDT) digital measurement and its role in objective wound assessment.
• Hands-on Demos and What's Next: Learn how near-infrared spectroscopy tissue oxygenation imaging will be incorporated into this integrated platform, and how to integrate imaging into the clinical workflow through hands on demonstrations..

Sponsored by MolecuLight.

abstracts.title: This workshop will focus on providing clinicians with actionable tips, techniques, and insights for effectively applying compression therapy to difficult-to-treat legs. While clinicians may grasp the theoretical aspects of compression kits during training, applying this knowledge to complex, real-world cases can be a significant challenge. This session aims to bridge that gap.

abstracts.title: Key Features of the Session:
abstracts.abstract:
Key Features of the Session:

•	Real-World Case Studies: Clinicians will engage with actual case scenarios that present unique challenges when applying compression therapy.

•	Practical Tips and Tricks: Gain insight into proven methods and techniques for improving the success of compression therapy, especially in tough cases.

•	Interactive Learning: The session will include interactive elements like live Q&A, trivia, and participant-driven discussion to reinforce key concepts and foster engagement.

•	Tribal Knowledge: We'll highlight the most effective tips and tricks from experienced clinicians, providing real-world solutions that participants can apply immediately in their practice.


Sponsored by Urgo Medical North America.

abstracts.title: Integrating Palliation into Your Wound Care Practice
abstracts.abstract: This session will review how many caregivers only consider palliative wound care for chronic wound patients with advanced disease or those at the end of life. However, the recommended approaches discussed here apply to a much broader population—patients for whom the focus should shift from healing to improving function and quality of life.

abstracts.title: Wound Care Business Navigator: Mobile Health and Wound Care Panel Discussion
abstracts.abstract: This session will explore how mobile wound care is transforming post-acute and long-term care by delivering advanced wound management directly to the patient’s environment. Attendees will learn about the regulatory and business essentials for launching or expanding a mobile wound care service. The session will also cover how to navigate reimbursement systems to effectively deliver care in these challenging settings.

abstracts.title: Evidence Based Adjunctive Care: Update on HBOT and NPWT
abstracts.abstract: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT) and negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) are widely used as adjunctive aids in healing difficult wounds. This session will focus on the latest clinical advances in both modalities, reconcile HBOTs conflicting evidence of benefit, and provide current reimbursement requirements to ensure their appropriate and effective use.

abstracts.title: PAWSIC: Patients' Voice: Communication to Understand Patient Needs
abstracts.abstract: Communication between the patient and clinician forms the cornerstone of effective healthcare. However, overuse of technical language, lack of attention to health literacy, and barriers to access can lead to misunderstandings and hinder care. This session will guide attendees on how to improve both written and verbal communication to meet patients where they are and enhance overall care quality.

abstracts.title: Greg Schultz Memorial Session: Wound Scene Investigation
abstracts.abstract: This session returns as a staple of SAWC Spring! Clinicians are often faced with complex and difficult-to-heal wounds and are tasked with identifying and overcoming barriers to healing. A diverse panel will present clinical cases, discuss underlying pathology and causes, and share specific management strategies to address these challenges—bringing real-world cases from the “bedside” to the “bench” and back again.

abstracts.title: John A. Boswick Award and Lectureship: Applying Systems Theory to Wound Healing
abstracts.abstract:
The annual John Boswick Award is given for global, selfless leadership in improving the scientific understanding or care of persons with wounds, lifelong achievement, and dedication in the field of wound care.

A wound’s impact extends far beyond the patient, creating a “wound web” that complicates healing across families, clinicians, and systems. This session explores some of the universal challenges of wound care and uses systems theory to reimagine pathways toward transformative, lasting change.

abstracts.title: SAWC Oral Abstracts
abstracts.abstract: This session will feature podium presentations from the highest scoring abstracts submitted to SAWC. Each presenting author will present key results of their research, followed by Q&A time with the audience, moderated by a member of the SAWC Oral Abstract Review Committee.

abstracts.title: Appropriate Use of Skin Substitutes
abstracts.abstract: This session will address how skin substitutes (cellular/tissue-based products) are an adjunctive therapy to consider after a patient fails to improve with the standard of care. These products can be very useful in achieving wound closure, but only in specific situations. The use of these products has come under increasing scrutiny by CMS and other insurers due to concerns over fraud, waste, and abuse. Attendees will review appropriate indications and suggested documentation language to support audits and prior authorization requests.

abstracts.title: Wound Care Business Navigator: Case Studies in Reimbursement
abstracts.abstract: This advanced session will dissect complex reimbursement challenges encountered by wound/ulcer management professionals, as well as their billers and coders. Together we will critically analyze the documentation, coding, and billing pitfalls that may cause claim denials or repayments after an audit. We will cover topics such as appropriate modifiers, procedure-to-procedure edits, debridement, and application of cellular and/or tissue-based products,  and application of autologous blood-derived products. Attendees will gain sophisticated strategies to receive appropriate payment and to keep their payment after an audit.

abstracts.title: Healing in Action: Reclaiming the Mission of Mobile Wound Management
abstracts.abstract: Amid growing scrutiny of mobile wound care practices, this roundtable reclaims the true mission of mobile wound management—delivering compassionate, evidence-based healing to patients who face barriers accessing traditional care. Expert faculty will examine how mobile models can expand access, enhance outcomes, and reduce hospital readmissions through ethical, accountable, and patient-centered care delivery. Discussion will focus on reinforcing credibility within the field, ensuring appropriate use of advanced therapies, and aligning clinical practices with principles of integrity and equity. Participants will gain practical strategies to communicate the value of mobile wound management to payers, providers, and patients, redefining it as a vital component of comprehensive wound care.

abstracts.title: RAPID FIRE: WCCC: Key Lessons from Real-World DFU Data
abstracts.abstract: This session highlights findings from the WCCC Natural History Project, a large-scale analysis of real-world data on patients with diabetic foot ulcers. The discussion will focus on how the patients seen in everyday practice differ from those enrolled in clinical trials and what this means for trial design, standards of care, and patient outcomes. Attendees will gain practical insights into using real-world evidence to better align research with the patients they treat every day.

abstracts.title: RAPID FIRE: Atypical Wounds: Focus on Treatment
abstracts.abstract: Atypical wounds are frequently encountered in wound care practice and often present diagnostic and therapeutic challenges beyond standard protocols. While accurate diagnosis is essential, successful management requires familiarity with tailored treatment strategies that may differ from conventional wound care. This session will highlight practical, case-based approaches and advanced modalities to equip clinicians with effective tools for treating complex and atypical wounds.

Attendees will leave with actionable insights they can implement as soon as Monday morning to improve their clinic’s financial efficiency.

abstracts.abstract: The medical-legal environment remains active, and the risk of litigation is ever-present. This session will discuss practical strategies to minimize the risk of adverse legal outcomes in wound care practice, helping clinicians reduce liability while maintaining high standards of care.

abstracts.title: Wound Care Business Navigator: RAPID FIRE: Reimbursement Insights for Financial Success of Hospital Owned Outpatient Wound/Ulcer Management Provider-Based Departments
abstracts.abstract: As the demand for advanced wound/ulcer management continues to grow, multi-disciplinary hospital owned outpatient wound/ulcer management provider-based departments (PBDs) are uniquely positioned to improve patient outcomes and to contribute to the hospital’s financial performance. This session will explore the business fundamentals of establishing and optimizing a successful PBD.  Attendees will gain insight into key operational components including staff selection, product selection for conservative and advanced care, diagnostic devices,  electronic health records,  coding, and billing. This session is ideal for administrators, physicians, and clinicians involved in PBD development or management.

abstracts.title: RAPID FIRE: Reassessing Paradigms in Wound Healing
abstracts.abstract: This session will critically evaluate commonly held ideas, beliefs, paradigms, and truths to assess whether existing data and evidence substantiate what is presently regarded as uncontroversial. Attendees will leave with a renewed perspective on wound-healing practices and a clearer understanding of how evidence can challenge and refine clinical decision-making.

abstracts.abstract: The expert panel in this session will explore the critical roles of prehabilitation and rehabilitation in optimizing wound healing and achieving sustained remission. Attendees will learn evidence-based strategies to prepare patients for successful wound management and implement post-healing interventions that can prevent recurrence and enhance long-term quality of life.

abstracts.title: Wound Imaging: Today and Tomorrow
abstracts.abstract: A variety of imaging and spectroscopy techniques have been developed to measure wound size, vascularization, blood oxygenation, bacterial load, and more. This session will discuss both the opportunities and limitations of wound imaging approaches that are currently in clinical use, as well as those still under development.

abstracts.title: Black Pearls: Common Myths in Wound Healing
abstracts.abstract: There are a vast number of devices, biologics, and drugs used in wound treatment—yet only a limited number are supported by strong evidence. This session will take a critical look at widely used products such as negative pressure wound therapy, silver-impregnated dressings, grafts, bacterial imaging, and topical gels. A thorough evidence-based review will be presented, contrasting clinical data with common marketing claims.

abstracts.title: Beyond the Flame: Caring for Patients with Burn Wounds
abstracts.abstract: Each year, millions of patients sustain burns through various mechanisms. These injuries lead to unique physiological changes that require specialized care to optimize healing and reduce complications. This session will provide a comprehensive overview of burn wound management across the entire care continuum.

abstracts.title: Each year, approximately 100,000 people in the United States undergo ostomy surgery, with nearly 1 million individuals currently living with an ostomy—about 1 in every 500 Americans. With rising rates of early-onset cancer and inflammatory bowel disease, more young patients are undergoing this procedure. Whether temporary or permanent, up to 35% of patients experience complications in the first year. This session will explore how effective ostomy care can improve clinical outcomes and long-term quality of life.

abstracts.title: Hot Topics in Pressure Injury: Skin Failure, Staging, and Regulations
abstracts.abstract: Pressure injuries remain a major quality focus for institutions and regulatory bodies. Despite years of discussion, there is still no standardized or widely adopted definition of skin failure, and no ICD-10 codes currently exist for this condition. Moreover, the National Quality Forum and CMS continue to misunderstand pressure injury etiology, leading to increasingly stringent reporting requirements that may negatively affect facilities—and potentially extend into home care. This session will examine these critical issues, including updates on staging, reclassification of harm events, and how these evolving standards impact clinical practice.

abstracts.title: Debridement is the cornerstone of successful wound healing. This session brings together a multi-disciplinary panel to explore the essential role of removing nonviable tissue, callus, and debris from the wound bed and wound edge to create an optimal environment for healing. This session will address how, when, and where different debridement methods should be performed — and when they shouldn’t — based on the patient, wound type, and clinical setting. Clinician expertise and training requirements, including those for allied health professionals, such physical therapists and nurses, will also be reviewed. Video vignettes will be featured, showcasing real-world scenarios and illustrating key principles and outcomes across care settings. Walk away with practical insights and expert tips on effective, evidence-based debridement to apply to your practice.
